An unseasonably dry July was taking its toll on non-irrigated crops in Kent and Sussex Counties in Delaware. Every summer crop was affected, with the worst damage to corn. Non-irrigated yields on corn fields were down as much as 50 to 100 percent. In Kent County, total crop losses on some farms reached 80 percent and in Sussex County, 60 percent. Unseasonably dry weather also led to an explosion in the spider mite and grasshopper populations which further damaged the crops. Even farms that had irrigation capabilities incurred additional costs because of the relatively high price of diesel fuel. In Sussex County, fish were dieing in Trussum Pond and Haven Lake because of low water levels. On a county-wide average, both Kent and Sussex Counties received less than half of their normal July rainfall. Sussex County received 1.5 inches and Kent County 1.9 inches. Normal rainfall is 4.2 inches. Specifically Georgetown's (Sussex County) July precipitation total was 1.68 inches.

An unseasonably hot and humid air mass affected Delaware on the 25th. While high temperatures were mainly in the lower to mid 90s, dew point temperatures averaged in the mid to upper 70s. This produced afternoon heat indices of 105F to 110F which were similar only to August 8th as the highest of the summer. A cold front moved through the area early on the 26th and limited this event to only one day.||Highest temperatures included 97 degrees in Blackbird (New Castle County), Redden and Prime Hook (Sussex County), 96 degrees in Sandtown (Kent County) and Greenwood (Sussex County), 95 degrees in Georgetown (Sussex County) and Newark (New Castle County), 91 degrees in Wilmington (New Castle County) and 88 degrees in Bethany Beach (Sussex County).

An approaching cold front helped trigger severe thunderstorms over Sussex County during the night of the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down several trees in both Lewes and Rehoboth Beach. Half inch hail also fell in Rehoboth Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front helped trigger severe thunderstorms over Sussex County during the night of the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down numerous trees in and around Georgetown. A bit farther to the east, the same thunderstorm snapped and/or downed eight utility poles on Southbound Delaware State Route 1 just north of the Indian River Inlet Bridge. Motorists on the roadway were stranded because of the downed poles. The downed poles also halted access in and out of Inlet Road and the Indian River Marina. Delaware State Route 1 was not completely reopened until the 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front helped trigger thunderstorms that were prolific lightning producers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced about 2,300 cloud-to-surface lightning strikes in Sussex County and the nearby waters between 11 p.m. EDT and Midnight EDT. Two lightning strikes caused damage. Lightning struck the chimney of an unoccupied unit of a townhouse complex in Port Lewes. The ensuing fire spread along the exterior of the unit to the adjoining unit in the complex. The residents of that unit were evacuated. Four fire companies battled the blaze. Damage was estimated at $150,000. No serious injuries were reported. In Rehoboth Beach, the fire station and city hall were struck by lightning. The strike fried the 911 system. The combination of wind and lightning caused about 7,000 homes and businesses to lose power in Sussex County. All power was restored by 3 a.m. EDT on the 17th.|

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front helped trigger severe thunderstorms over Sussex County during the night of the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down several trees in Ocean View.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heat wave brought unseasonably hot weather to Delaware on July 8th through the 10th. The highest temperatures were on the 9th and 10th and were mostly in the mid to upper 90s. The combination of the heat and humidity produced afternoon heat indices of around 100F both afternoons. Cloudiness that preceded a cold front on the 11th prevented most places from reaching 90F again. During the heat wave, Delaware Electric Cooperative requested customers to use less power. Four cooling stations were opened in Sussex County. Portable air condition sales jumped about 50 percent.||Highest temperatures included 99 degrees in Prime Hook (Sussex County), 98 degrees in Dover and Milford (Kent County), 97 degrees in Sandtown and Smyrna (Kent County), Greenwood and Seaford (Sussex County) and 96 degrees in Blackbird and Middletown (New Castle County) and Bethany Beach (Sussex County).
A slow moving cold front helped trigger a severe thunderstorm in New Castle County during the evening of the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down large tree limbs and dropped penny size hail in Townsend.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front helped trigger thunderstorms that were  prolific cloud-to-ground lightning producers during the late afternoon and early evening of the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A series of thunderstorms over a period of three hours produced about 1100 cloud-to-surface lightning strikes in Sussex County and Delaware Bay from 4 p.m. EDT through 7 p.m. EDT on the 16th. About ten structures were struck by lightning in and around Lewes. The worst reported property damage was the ensuing fire that occurred after a lightning strike to a Brighton Drive home. The fire damage was estimated at $50,000. The lightning strike in the Sussex East Mobile Home Park started a shed fire which spread to a home, but was quickly extinguished. Two small fires were started by lightning strikes to homes in the Eagle Point Landing Development. Another lightning strike occurred at an apartment building at the Village of Five Points development. Lightning struck a Home Depot twice on Delaware State Route 1. Lightning also struck the Furniture Galley on Delaware State Route 1 and Practically Yours on U.S. Route 9. No serious injuries were reported with any of the fires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching warm front helped produce severe thunderstorms and also caused some lightning damage across northern Delaware.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms tore down a few trees and wires across northern New Castle County from Prices Corner in Christina Hundred eastward. The combination of lightning and damaging winds caused about 5,200 homes and businesses in New Castle County to lose power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front helped trigger a severe thunderstorm and lightning damage in Sussex County on the afternoon of the 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a few homes across Sussex County between 330 p.m. EDT and 415 p.m. EDT on the 28th. A lightning strike and ensuing fire damaged a Kelley Circle home near Seaford. In addition to wind damage elsewhere, lightning struck a home on Clayton Street in Frankford. The ensuing fire damaged the outer wall of the home. Three fire companies assisted in battling the blaze. The worst reported damage occurred to a home struck by lightning in Lewes. Lightning struck a home in the Bay Crossing Community on Anchor Way. Four fire companies fought the ensuing blaze which caused heavy damage to the second floor and roof of the home. No serious injuries were reported with any of the fires.

Unseasonably dry weather in September continued to take its toll on non-irrigated crops across Delaware. For the state, it was the 5th driest September on record dating back to 1895. On a county weighted average, September rainfall ranged from 0.4 inches in New Castle County, to 1.2 inches in Kent County. Normal is about 3.7 inches.||The crop damage was already done. On September 11th, the United States Department of Agriculture declared the entire state a drought disaster area. This made farmers who suffered more than 30 percent crop losses and unable to secure loans from convential banks to be eligible for low interest loans. It was estimated that 90 percent of all Delaware farmers suffered at least 30 percent crop losses. The last drought disaster occurred in 2002.||The corn harvest was expected to be 40 percent less than 2006, the largest year over year decline of any state. The corn yield per acre  |was expected to be the second lowest in the nation. The soybean harvest was affected also as it was forecasted to be 32 percent less than 2006. It was expected to be the lowest soybean harvest since 1970. Even farms that had irrigation capabilities, the high cost of electricity and diesel fuel cut into their profits. The reduced corn harvest forced the Delaware poultry industry to have to import feed from other states. Hay fields were also dry and there were continued problems with spider mites. One of the few benefits of the dry weather was that it reduced powdery mildew problems on pumpkins and there were no muddy field problems.||The drought started to have an affect on stream and water supplies. Shallow groundwater wells were near record lows. Deeper wells were falling slower. Newark had to start using its back-up reservoir for water supplies because of the reduced flow along the White Clay Creek. Pennsylvania started to release 5 million gallons of water a day from the Marsh Creek Reservoir to protect downstream ecosystems and water users along the Brandywine Creek. Dry conditions allowed salt levels to move farther upstream than normal along tidal sections of the Christina River and White Clay Creek. An inflatable dam was used to protect the fresh water supply of United Water of Delaware. The landscaping industry considered this the 2nd or 3rd worst growing season in the past decade.||Actual monthly precipitation totals included 0.49 inches at the New Castle County Airport, 4th driest on record. Normal is 4.01 inches. In Georgetown, the monthly precipitation total was 1.14 inches. Normal is 3.99 inches.
